figures.gloss: The biblical monarch reimagined as the ultimate master of the invisible world. According to grimoric legend, he bound the seventy-two rebellious spirits into a brazen vessel and forced them to build his temple, establishing the foundational paradigm of conjuration.
figures.facet: He is not a prophet of moral law here, but a cosmic engineer who weaponizes the demonic. His authority rests on operational knowledge, not just piety, modeling the sorcerer as a commander.
figures.provenance: The 'Testament of Solomon' (1st-5th c.) and the 'Lemegeton' (17th c.).

figures.gloss: The first principal spirit of the Goetia, ruling as a King in the East. He appears with three heads—a toad, a man, and a cat. He speaks with a hoarse voice, granting the power of invisibility and vast occult wisdom.
figures.facet: A stark demotion of the ancient Levantine storm god into a captive servitor. He retains royal titles but is leashed to the geometric whims of mortal sorcerers for parlor tricks and hidden knowledge.

figures.gloss: A mighty duke appearing as a hurtful angel riding an infernal beast, carrying a viper. He teaches liberal sciences, discovers hidden secrets, and truthfully answers regarding the fall of the angels—while protesting his own unjust exile from heaven.
figures.facet: Astaroth must be kept at a distance with a magic ring because of his noxious breath. He embodies the tragic, poisonous decay of older deities rotting in the grimoric prison.
figures.provenance: The 'Ars Goetia'. Historically mutated and gender-flipped from the Canaanite goddess Astarte.

figures.gloss: A great king with three heads—a bull, a man, and a ram—breathing fire and riding a dragon. He commands seventy-two legions, teaches mathematics, reveals treasures, and grants the Ring of Virtues, but ruthlessly tests the exorcist's courage.
figures.facet: He is a deeply transactional entity who demands respect. If the operator commands him while bareheaded, Asmoday will deceive him and overthrow all his workings, proving his obedience is highly conditional.
figures.provenance: Rooted in the Zoroastrian demon of wrath, Aeshma Daeva, passing through the Book of Tobit into grimoire lore.

figures.gloss: The physical and psychic architecture of goetic magic. The magician stands inside a nine-foot circle inscribed with divine names for absolute protection, while the spirit is forced to manifest within an external triangle to be constrained and commanded.
figures.facet: It establishes a literal, geometric border between the divine microcosm (the operator) and the chaotic macrocosm (the demon). It is not for worship, but serves as a spiritual quarantine zone.
figures.provenance: Standardized in European grimoires like the 'Heptameron' and the 'Lesser Key of Solomon'.

figures.gloss: The unique geometric emblem belonging to each of the seventy-two spirits. It is drawn on parchment or specific metals according to the demon's planetary rank and worn upon the breast of the exorcist to compel absolute obedience.
figures.facet: The sigil is treated as the demon's true name rendered in spatial form. Holding or wearing it is functionally equivalent to holding the spirit by the throat.
figures.provenance: Codified in the 'Ars Goetia', drawing on older astrological and talismanic traditions.

figures.gloss: The endless permutations of the Abrahamic God's names—like Tetragrammaton, Adonai, and Agla—shouted by the exorcist to break the demon's will. The magic relies entirely on the operator successfully assuming the identity and untouchable authority of the Creator.
figures.facet: The magician does not ask or pray; they threaten the demons with cosmic annihilation. It is an aggressive, weaponized hijacking of divine authority strictly for pragmatic ends.
figures.provenance: Derived from Jewish Kabbalah and Christian mysticism, bastardized into grimoric formulas.

figures.gloss: The legendary sealed brazen urn into which King Solomon cast the seventy-two demonic kings and their legions, sealing it with divine lead. The Babylonians later opened it hoping for treasure, unwittingly unleashing the spirits back into the world.
figures.facet: It represents the temporary imprisonment of chaos by human civilization, acting as a mythological lockbox that is inevitably broken by mortal greed.
figures.provenance: Originates in the pseudepigraphical 'Testament of Solomon', carried into Islamic folklore and the 'Ars Goetia'.

figures.gloss: The ninth spirit of the Ars Goetia, a great king who appears riding a dromedary, crowned and preceded by a roaring host with trumpets and cymbals; he teaches all arts and sciences, reveals hidden things, and can bind other spirits and familiars into the operator's service.
figures.facet: The Goetia's own hierarch among hierarchs: his power to compel other spirits makes him a captive middle-manager of the infernal bureaucracy, ruling within the very leash he wears.
figures.provenance: The 'Ars Goetia' (Lemegeton, Bk. I); Johann Weyer's 'Pseudomonarchia Daemonum' (1577).

figures.gloss: The sixty-eighth spirit, said to have been created immediately after Lucifer: he appears as a beautiful angel seated in a chariot of fire, speaking fair and distributing honors and senatorial favor, but must be bound to a fixed place with offerings and sacrifices lest he lie and deceive the operator.
figures.facet: His name predates the grimoire by well over a millennium — 'Belial' names worthlessness itself in the Hebrew Bible and leads the Sons of Darkness in the Qumran War Scroll — arguably the deepest textual root of any single spirit on the Goetia's list.
figures.provenance: The 'Ars Goetia'; the name's biblical usage (Deuteronomy 13:13, 2 Corinthians 6:15) and its role as chief of darkness in the Dead Sea Scrolls' 'War Scroll' and 'Community Rule'.

figures.gloss: The thirteenth spirit, a terrible and mighty king riding a pale horse, heralded by trumpets and melodious music before appearing in fury; the exorcist must hold a hazel wand and wear a silver ring, commanding him with courtesy until his rage subsides, whereupon he procures love between man and woman.
figures.facet: A set-piece in the text's own operator etiquette: rage that must be met with ceremony rather than force, a spirit whose obedience is won by protocol rather than power.

figures.gloss: The twenty-sixth spirit, a great and strong duke appearing as a three-headed dragon with wings and a dog's tail, or in human form at command; he moves the bodies of the dead to new graves, gathers spirits about their tombs, gives riches, and makes men eloquent and wise.
figures.facet: A demon whose portfolio is literally graveyard logistics — the goetic bureaucracy of captive spirits extended even into the rearrangement of corpses.

figures.gloss: The thirty-sixth spirit, appearing in the form of an owl or a raven before taking human shape at the exorcist's command; he teaches the science of astronomy and the virtues of herbs, plants, and precious stones.
figures.facet: One of the Goetia's few purely pedagogical spirits: his threat is nil and his currency is natural knowledge, aligning him with scholarship rather than temptation or wrath.

figures.gloss: The third spirit, described by the text itself as being 'of a good nature'; invoked chiefly for divination, to declare things past and to come and to discover anything hidden or lost.
figures.facet: The Goetia's own built-in exception — a spirit whose good nature is stated outright in the text, complicating the list's otherwise uniform frame of malice bound and leashed.

figures.gloss: The composite 17th-century grimoire in five books — Ars Goetia, Ars Theurgia-Goetia, Ars Paulina, Ars Almadel, and Ars Notoria — that assembled the seventy-two spirits, their seals, and their evocation protocols into the tradition's single defining compendium.
figures.facet: The text that turns scattered demonological lore into a manual: a bureaucratic index of the infernal, complete with seals, ranks, and standard operating procedure.
figures.provenance: 17th-century composite manuscript tradition drawing on Weyer's 'Pseudomonarchia Daemonum' (1577); first printed in English by S.L. MacGregor Mathers and Aleister Crowley (1904), fuller critical edition by Joseph Peterson (2001).

figures.gloss: The Greek pseudepigraphal narrative (1st-5th c. CE) in which King Solomon, aided by a ring given by the archangel Michael, interrogates a succession of demons about their names, powers, and the angels that thwart them, and binds them to labor on the construction of the Jerusalem Temple.
figures.facet: The root document of the whole paradigm this tradition runs on: binding demons to forced labor by name and ring predates the Lemegeton's seventy-two kings by over a millennium.
figures.provenance: The 'Testament of Solomon' (Greek pseudepigraphon, 1st-5th c. CE; C.C. McCown's critical edition, 1922).

figures.gloss: The seventy-two angels derived from the three verses Exodus 14:19-21 (each yielding a three-letter Name), traditionally paired one-to-one against the seventy-two Goetic spirits to compel and balance them, per the recension preserved in Dr. Thomas Rudd's manuscripts.
figures.facet: Where the Ars Goetia alone reads as raw demon-commanding, the Shem HaMephorash pairing reframes the whole operation as angelic supervision — every captive demon assigned its own warden.
figures.provenance: Kabbalistic 72-fold Name tradition (Exodus 14:19-21); Dr. Thomas Rudd's 17th-century manuscripts, edited by Stephen Skinner and David Rankine as 'The Goetia of Dr Rudd' (2007).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: Solomon is the architect of the vessel that bound the infernal multitude.
Intra-tradition bond: The geometric boundary is activated and enforced exclusively by the spoken Words of Power.
Intra-tradition bond: The sigil is the exact, mechanical key by which a spirit like Bael is compelled to obedience.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Bael is the direct, demonized medieval survival of the Canaanite storm god Baal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Astaroth is the demonized, gender-flipped derivation of the goddess Astarte, cognate with Inanna.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Goetic operator literally speaks as Yahweh, assuming His authority and names to command the demons.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent profound adversarial forces; Asmoday stems specifically from the Zoroastrian Aeshma Daeva, a lieutenant of Angra Mainyu.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both use a physical, constructed anchor to trap, direct, and utilize an autonomous spirit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a bounded, ritually purified space that protects the practitioner from chaotic external forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Legendary kings who ruled over demons and humanity alike during a golden age before falling from grace.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a sealed, chthonic prison where defeated elder deities or rebellious spirits are kept out of the mortal order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figures harness a dangerous, forbidden power to build human civilization.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both demand exact, unforgiving spatial and ritual orientation to correctly interface with the unseen and avoid disaster.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both enforce the inviolable cosmic law that binds entities through oaths and true speech.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the forces of illusion and temptation that the spiritual operator must master and bind.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are monstrous, fallen rulers of a corrupt material or astral hierarchy, retaining arrogance despite their cosmic demotion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are dangerous, monstrous, or captured entities that teach humanity secret metalworking and crafts.
The Ars Goetia's demon-binding magician is built directly on the biblical temple-building king, his name and legend borrowed wholesale for the grimoire's frame narrative.
Intra-tradition bond: Solomon is the legendary compiler-authority all five books of the Lemegeton claim as their source.
Intra-tradition bond: the Lemegeton's Ars Goetia is the text that codifies all seventy-two demonic seals.
Intra-tradition bond: the Testament is the root narrative of Solomon's demon-binding that the Lemegeton later systematizes into seventy-two named kings.
Intra-tradition bond: the brazen vessel legend is native to the Testament, later folded into the Ars Goetia's own frame story.
Intra-tradition bond: Paimon is one of the seventy-two kings Solomon is said to have bound into the brazen vessel.
Intra-tradition bond: Belial is likewise numbered among the seventy-two spirits of the vessel legend.
Intra-tradition bond: both systems weaponize a divine Name against the spirits — the Shem HaMephorash's 72-fold angelic Name is the Kabbalistic counterpart to the Words of Power the exorcist shouts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the 72-fold expansion of the divine Name used to bind and balance the Goetic spirits derives directly from Kabbalistic permutation of the Tetragrammaton.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both charter myths center on the same Temple; the Testament makes the bound demons its literal builders, a detail Masonic legend of Solomon's Temple omits while sharing the same architectural frame.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: parallel pseudepigraphal grimoire traditions attributing magical dominion to a legendary biblical figure — Solomon's seals in the European goetic current, Moses's in the current absorbed into hoodoo.

Aeshma Daeva passes by name directly into Second Temple Jewish demonology as Ashmedai, then into the medieval grimoire tradition as King Asmoday — a traceable chain from Gathic daeva to ceremonial-magic demon.

Same King Solomon, opposite technologies for the same building project: Masonic legend keeps him the wise Grand Master directing free craftsmen who raise the Temple by skill, while the Testament/Goetia tradition has him bind seventy-two demons under a magical seal to build the same Temple by compulsion.

dossier.essence: The Western tradition of 'conjure-and-command' ritual magic — evoking spirits or demons and compelling them by the authority of divine names. It is best known from the early-modern grimoires, above all the 'Ars Goetia,' first book of the 17th-century 'Lemegeton' (Lesser Key of Solomon), which catalogues seventy-two spirits a magician can summon and constrain. Pseudonymously credited to King Solomon, it descends from late-antique and medieval Solomonic magic and continues in living practice among modern occultists.
dossier.cosmos: It presupposes a broadly Christian cosmos — God above, ranks of angels, fallen and demonic spirits below — in which each of the seventy-two spirits holds a rank (king, duke, president, marquis), commands legions, and answers to planetary and directional correspondences and particular powers (knowledge, favor, the finding of treasure). Many are demoted older deities: Bael from the Canaanite Baal, Astaroth from the goddess Astarte. Goetia offers no rival doctrine of salvation or afterlife; it is an operative technology working within the surrounding religion, not against it.
dossier.practice: The operator stands within a protective circle inscribed with divine names and forces the spirit to manifest in a triangle outside it, using the spirit's seal or sigil, the brazen vessel, incense, and escalating conjurations (with 'curses' if it resists). The tradition is intensely textual and procedural — exact timings, purifications, and materials — transmitted through manuscript grimoires copied and elaborated across centuries; the magician's stance is command, not supplication.
dossier.canon: The 'Lemegeton' or 'Lesser Key of Solomon' (compiled 17th c.), especially its first part the 'Ars Goetia'; Johann Weyer's 'Pseudomonarchia Daemonum' (1577), an earlier list of the spirits; the late-antique 'Testament of Solomon' as ancestor; and cognate grimoires such as the 'Heptameron' and the 'Key of Solomon' (Clavicula Salomonis).
dossier.reflects: The coercive, instrumental pole of the religious impulse — the human being not as worshipper but as commander, bending unseen powers to will through exact technique and the borrowed authority of God's own names.
sources: Owen Davies, 'Grimoires: A History of Magic Books' (2009)
Richard Kieckhefer, 'Magic in the Middle Ages' (1989)
Joseph H. Peterson (ed.), 'The Lesser Key of Solomon: Lemegeton Clavicula Salomonis' (2001)
Pablo A. Torijano, 'Solomon the Esoteric King: From King to Magus, Development of a Tradition' (2002)
